english: Cs. = kloṅ (klong); one Lex. = dkyil (dkyil); v. kloṅ (klong).
bka-mčid (bka' mchid), ysuṅ-mčid (gsung mchid), W. *mol-čid* (*mol chid*) resp the talk, discourse, speech (of an honoured person) Cs.; mčid-lán (mchid lan) answer to such speech Mil.

resp. sku-mčin (sku mchin) the liver; mčin-drí (mchin dri), mčin-rí (mchin ri) the midriff or diaphragm; mčin-k'a (mchin kha) liver-coloured; mčin-nán (mchin nan) 'liver-pressing', first breakfast, because according to popular belief water rises from the human liver in the morning, which is depressed and appeased by taking some food; mčin-nán byed-pa (mchin nan byed pa) to breakfast.

1. fishing-hook Dzl., mčil-pas (mchil pas) nya (nya) ₒčór-ba ('chor ba) to fish with a hook, to angle Cs. — 2. a little bird, W. *čirpa* (*cirpa*), Ts. *čil-pig* (*chil pig*); *čí-pa skyú-wo* (*chi pa skyu wo*) W. sparrow; mčil-kra (mchil khra) sparrow-hawk; mčil-mgó (mchil mgo) a fabulous stone, like a bird's head, supposed to possess a variety of marvelous qualities.

1. W. *mčil-mág* (*mchil mag*), resp. ljags-mčil (ljags mchil), ljags-čúb (ljags chub) spittle, prob. also other similar fluids Lt.; ₒdór-ba ('dor ba) (W. *púṅ-ce* (*pung ce*)) to spit; mčil-lúd (mchil lud) (W. *mčil-ldúd* (*mchil ldud*)) morbid saliva, e.g. of people affected with a cough or with hectic fevers; mčil-snábs (mchil snabs) prob. id.; mčil-snód (mchil snod), resp. žal-bzéd (zhal bzed), spitting-box; mčil-zúm (mchil zum), mčil-bkáb (mchil bkab) W. slavering-bib or cloth. — 2. = mčil-lhám (mchil lham) Tar. 72, 9?

shoe, boot, mčil-lhám (mchil lham) rnyis (gnyis) ₒdor-ba ('dor ba) to lose both shoes Wdn.; mčil-lhám-mk'an (mchil lham mkhan) shoemaker, cobbler, seller of boots; mčil-lhám-gyi (mchil lham gyi) yú-ba (yu ba) the leg of a boot Cs.

1. also mčis-lágs-pa (mchis lags pa), eleg. for yod-pa (yod pa), to be, to be there, to exist, du mčis (du mchis) how much is there, how many are there? Cs.; sú-la (su la) dám-pai (dam pa'i) čos (chos) mčis-pa (mchis pa) whoever has the holy doctrine Dzl.; yul (yul) dbús-nas (dbus nas) mčis-so (mchis so) (he) is (comes) from the country Ü Dzl. — 2. pf. of mči-ba (mchi ba) 1. lam (lam) riṅ-po-nas (ring po nas) mčis-te (mchis te) having come from afar. 2. žes (zhes) mčis-pa (mchis pa) so-called.
1. eleg. dwelling, abode, domicile; also when speaking modestly of one's own dwelling: bdág-gi (bdag gi) mčis-bráṅ (mchis brang) my humble roof Dzl. — 2. Lex. wife, partner.

1. lip, ya-mču (ya mchu) upper lip, ma-mču (ma mchu) lower lip; mču (mchu) btud (btud) mk'as (mkhas) Wil. prob.: one must be wise in lowering the lips, i.e. one must yield, giving up pouting; k'a-mčú (kha mchu), resp. žal-mčú (zhal mchu) 1. lip 2. word, voice (?) Sch. 3. quarrel, strife, k'a-mčú (kha mchu) rgyal-pám (rgyal pham) ji-ltar (ji ltar) byuṅ (byung) žé-na (zhe na) if one asks, which are the details of the quarrel; *k'am-čú jhé'-pa* (*kham chu jhe' pa*) C. *gyág-pa* (*gyag pa*) Cs. to quarrel. — 2. beak or bill of birds, mčú-la (mchu la) tógs-te (togs te) gró-ba (gro ba) to fly, carrying something in the bill S. O.; mču-lto (mchu lto) (or ču-mdo? (chu mdo?)) W. id. — 3. n. of one of the lunar mansions, v. rgyu-skar (rgyu skar). Comp. mču-skyé (mchu skye) muzzle Sch. — mču-sgrós (mchu sgros) v. sgros (sgros). — mču-t'ór (mchu thor) Sch. (prob. a mis-print for mču-tór (mchu tor)) pustules, tubercular elevations on the lips. — mču-riṅs (mchu rings) long-beaked, n. of a bird, and also of an insect (a large musquito).

Cs. also mče-só (mche so), corner-tooth, canine tooth, eye-tooth, fang, tusk of an animal, mčé-la (mche la) ytsigs-pa (gtsigs pa), W. *žè-če* (*zhe che*), to show one's teeth, to grin; mče-ba-čan-gyi (mche ba can gyi) sde (sde) the class of the tusked animals, viz. the carnivora (lion, tiger, leopard), and the tusked pachydermata (elephant, boar etc.).

sku-mčed (sku mched), mčed-lčám (mched lcam), resp. for spun (spun), brother, sister; mčed (mched) rnyis (gnyis) my two brothers Dzl.; srás-mo (sras mo) lha-lčám (lha lcam) mčed (mched) bži (bzhi) four princesses, sisters; deï (de'i) mčed (mched) his illustrious brother, in reference to a king, prince etc. Glr.; esp. of gods: mčed (mched) bži (bzhi) four divine brothers Glr.; mčed-grógs (mched grogs), grogs-mčed (grogs mched) clerical brother, mčed-grógs (mched grogs) máṅ-po (mang po) tsógs-par (tshogs par) where many clerical brothers assemble; mčed-grógs (mched grogs) dam-tsig (dam tshig) yčig-pa (gcig pa) Thgr. betrothed brothers, religious brothers, = čos-spún (chos spun); also mčed-lčám (mched lcam) has this signification.
